terms.definition: attains, reaches, comes to understand; the active acquisition of prasāda through right practice—this is not passive grace but earned through discipline.
terms.roots: from adhi-gam (to go to, attain, understand)

terms.definition: having no other; without deviation or rival; absolute singularity of devotion where consciousness permits no competitors
impermanent, non-eternal; fleeting by nature, like all phenomena arising from prakṛti; the truth that all vṛttis are temporary processes
impermanent, non-eternal — the defining quality of all prakṛtic phenomena; that which cannot be relied upon for lasting satisfaction
Of one who is not connected; the unyoked mind, unstable by definition, executing without proper environment variables set.
O descendant of Bharata — appellation invoking Arjuna's paternal lineage; reminder of the greatness of the Bharata dynasty
Fixed mind, steady consciousness; the capacity to maintain unwavering focus; the mental state that survives input fluctuations.
Transcendental intelligence, discriminating wisdom; the faculty that distinguishes puruṣa from prakṛti; the debugger of consciousness.
terms.roots: from budh (to wake up, be aware of)

terms.definition: intelligence, discernment, faculty of judgment; the interface between puruṣa and prakṛti; the tool that can recognize its own source
terms.roots: from budh (to wake up, be aware of)

terms.definition: thinking of, meditating upon; continuous unbroken thought-flow toward the Supreme; active unceasing remembrance
terms.roots: from cint (to think); the dynamic of sustained contemplation

terms.definition: The embodied one; consciousness inhabiting material form. The witness, not the vessel.
terms.roots: from deha (body) + -in (possessive suffix); literally 'one who has a body'

terms.definition: while contemplating, pondering; the gerundive form indicating continuous mental engagement. In Sāṅkhya/Yoga psychology, the first vṛtti that externalizes consciousness—merely thinking about sense objects initiates the cascade toward attachment.
aversion, hatred, repulsion; the inverse attachment—pushing away what caused pain; both rāga and dveṣa bind equally, just different polarities of the same trap.
destruction, cessation, disappearance; the loss or removal of something unwanted; spiritual garbage collection
by the senses; the five senses of perception (jñānendriya) and five of action (karmendriya); the API endpoints through which prakṛti interfaces with experience.
From sense objects; the domain where senses seek engagement.
Worn out, old, decayed; that which has served its purpose and is now obsolete; ready to be released.
terms.roots: from jṝ (to decay, become old, wear out)

terms.definition: O son of Kuntī — appellation invoking Arjuna's maternal lineage; reminder of the duty that comes from noble birth
sense-object contacts — the interface where consciousness meets prakṛti; the API boundary where input triggers response
Restrained, curbed down; the senses actively withdrawn from objects like a skilled administrator recalling agents from the field.
of those always fixed in devotion; perpetually engaged; ever-connected in yoga
becomes well established, stands firm; pari- = completion, ava-sthā = to stand; the intelligence that attains stable, immovable discernment
worship properly from all sides; surround with service; complete adequate worship that encompasses the Lord
terms.roots: pari (around) + upās (to sit near/serve); circumambulatory devotion

terms.definition: Wisdom, intelligence, discriminative knowledge; the awakened buddhi that distinguishes puruṣa from prakṛti.
of the satisfied/pure-hearted; prasanna = clear, bright, satisfied; cetas = mind/heart/consciousness; one whose inner lake reflects truth without distortion
grace, mercy, tranquility; causeless favor of the Lord; the result of bhakti-yoga practice; also the sanctified remnants of offering to the Lord.
terms.roots: from pra-sad (to settle down, become clear, be pleased)

terms.definition: in the grace/mercy of the Lord; the settled state of prasāda; the location where divine favor rests and from which suffering is annihilated.
terms.roots: from pra (forth) + sad (to settle, sink down); that which settles firmly

terms.definition: Established, fixed, firmly situated; intelligence grounded in its true nature, like a system running without crashes or memory leaks.

withdrawal, retraction; the fifth limb of aṣṭāṅga yoga (YS II.54). Sense withdrawal occurs when the mind is fixed on its object, causing senses to automatically cease contact with their objects. Not repression but natural internalization when buddhi withdraws from prakṛtic toward puruṣic.
one falls down, is ruined, perishes; the inevitable consequence when the entire chain of saṁsāra executes—total loss of spiritual position when buddhi is destroyed.

attachment, passion, coloration of the mind; the condition where the mind is drawn toward objects based on remembered pleasure from past experiences; like legacy code that keeps executing because it worked once.
complete delusion, bewilderment; the loss of discriminative clarity when buddhi operates under dense tamas; follows anger in the downward spiral.
of all miseries; sarva = all, duḥkha = suffering/material distress (the threefold: adhyatmika, adhibhautika, adhidaivika)
Goes toward, accepts, attains; voluntary movement toward the new; the soul's acceptance of a new vehicle.
memory; specifically the capacity to remember one's true nature (puruṣa) and the connection to truth; distinct from ordinary recollection. When bewildered (smṛti-bhraṁśa), intelligence is lost.
you must tolerate/endure — active forbearance, not passive suffering; the austere patience that maintains practice through all conditions
equanimity, indifference, overlooking; one of the four attitudes in YS I.33, specifically toward the sukha/duḥkha of conditioned existence; the emotional Neutral gear
terms.roots: from upa- (near) + √īkṣ (to look, regard); looking with equanimity

terms.definition: I carry, I bear, I transport; the Lord's active taking of complete responsibility; personal promise
terms.roots: from vah (to carry, bear, convey); divine intervention

terms.definition: one whose nature follows regulated principles; disciplined freedom—the apparent paradox that structure enables liberation.
terms.roots: vidheya (to be followed, from vi-dhā) + ātmā (self)

terms.definition: Having abandoned, left behind; the act of release without regret or lingering attachment; casting off the worn-out.
terms.roots: from vi-hā (to leave behind, abandon, give up)

terms.definition: liberated, released, set free; the state of being completely untangled from the kleshas; root muc—to loosen, release.
sense objects; that which spreads out (vi-ṣaya) before consciousness; the target of sense engagement.
terms.roots: from viṣ (to be active, act) + -aya or vi- (apart) + sṛ (to spread)

terms.definition: Garments, clothes; temporary coverings that protect but are not the form itself; the body as temporary clothing for the soul.
terms.roots: from vas (to put on, wear); also related to vastra (garment)

terms.definition: acquisition and preservation; what is lacking plus what is possessed; divine logistics—supply and security
terms.roots: yoga (acquisition/connection) + kṣema (protection/welfare); the Lord's total responsibility

terms.type: adjective, past passive participle from yuj
terms.definition: Yoked, connected, joined; one who is in Kṛṣṇa consciousness; the 'compiled' soul whose consciousness runs on the proper kernel.
those that come and go — describing sensations as temporary visitors, not permanent residents
under the control of the self (ātman + vaśya); the senses operating as api endpoints the self controls, not as rogue processes hijacking attention.
terms.roots: ātman (self) + vaśya (to be controlled, from vaś)

terms.definition: quickly, immediately, very soon; the speed of transformation when conditions are ripe—not gradual evolution but sudden shift when prasāda settles.
terms.roots: from the same root as āśis (benediction); swiftness born of favor

terms.definition: Peace, tranquility; not mere absence of disturbance but a positive state of fullness rooted in satisfaction of the soul.
cold and heat — the classic paired opposites; all dvandva that disturb the undiscriminating mind
